responses.short_answer: I'd try it immediately if it reliably extracts and assigns action items — not just transcribes words. The hesitation comes down to two things: whether it handles Japanese accurately enough for business use, and whether deploying it at NTT Data would require a six-month IT security review that kills the value before I even test it.
responses.reasoning: My core belief is that every meeting needs a written artifact. Right now I spend 15–20 minutes after each meeting manually summarizing and sending action items in Slack — that's the exact friction I want removed. But I've been burned before by tools that produce beautiful transcripts with zero actionability, so the bar is 'does it correctly assign owners and deadlines,' not 'does it record audio.' The NTT Data environment is enterprise-grade, meaning client data is often discussed in meetings, so any tool that stores recordings on foreign cloud servers is a non-starter from a data governance standpoint before I even evaluate features. And frankly, if the Japanese language accuracy is at the level I've seen in some Western-first tools, the output would require more cleanup than just writing the summary myself.

responses.short_answer: If someone showed me hard numbers — time saved per week, fewer missed action items, measurable — I might consider it. But until then, no. Our meetings are short, on the gemba, and we settle things face to face. I don't see the gap this is filling.
responses.reasoning: Our division runs on 15-minute stand-up meetings at the line. Nobody takes elaborate notes because the decisions are immediate and simple. What would AI transcription do for that? If head office sent this down as another 'DX initiative,' I'd push back until someone showed me a real number — not a slide with a percentage and no denominator. I also worry about confidential production data sitting on some cloud server I've never inspected. That's not acceptable to me without a serious conversation about where the data goes.

responses.short_answer: Honestly, I'd try it immediately — meetings where nobody takes notes and decisions just... disappear is literally my number one operational nightmare. What would make me hesitate is if the pricing is opaque, if it doesn't handle Japanese properly, or if setup requires more than 10 minutes and an IT ticket.
responses.reasoning: We're 12 people and I'm in back-to-back calls half the day — English standups, Japanese partner meetings, investor pings. Right now I'm the de facto note-taker for half of them because nobody else does it consistently, which is insane for a COO. So the value prop clicks instantly for me. But I've been burned by tools that work beautifully in English demos and then completely fall apart when someone switches to Japanese mid-sentence, which happens constantly in our meetings. And the other thing — if I land on a pricing page and there's no clear startup plan or free tier, I'm gone in 30 seconds. I don't have time to 'contact sales for a quote.'

responses.short_answer: The thing that would genuinely move me is knowing it had cleared our bank's information security review — without that, I cannot even pilot it, regardless of how useful it looks. What would make me hesitate is everything I cannot verify: where the audio goes, who can access the transcript, whether a meeting about a client's portfolio situation is being stored on a foreign server I've never heard of.
responses.reasoning: I attend perhaps six to eight internal meetings a week, and I currently write notes by hand in my Hobonichi, then retype the key decisions into our shared drive that same evening — a habit I know is inefficient but cannot seem to let go of. If a tool could reliably capture action items and owners, I would genuinely be relieved. But at our branch, any software that touches meeting content must go through our IT governance process, and I've seen well-meaning colleagues get formally reprimanded for using unapproved cloud tools, even for seemingly harmless things. The compliance risk is not abstract to me. And honestly, even setting aside the formal process, I would be deeply uneasy about a vendor's AI processing conversations where we might be discussing client strategy or internal restructuring — that kind of material cannot be treated casually.

responses.short_answer: My interest would begin — and I mean begin, not end — with a clear data residency statement: where are meeting transcripts stored, for how long, and under what legal jurisdiction. If the vendor can demonstrate Japan-region hosting, robust access controls, and a credible data processing agreement compliant with Japanese privacy law, I will consider moving to a technical evaluation. What would make me hesitate is essentially everything that comes before that documentation is produced.
responses.reasoning: Our firm's meetings routinely contain client strategy, M&A discussions, and personnel matters — treating that audio or transcript as anything other than confidential business data would be a serious lapse in judgment on my part. I've already blocked three AI-note tools this fiscal year because consultants signed up with their work email before anyone read the terms of service; the default data routing was to US-based servers with no opt-out. What would genuinely move the needle for me is a vendor who opens the conversation with their ISO 27001 certification and a dedicated Japan data region, not a feature demo — I can read a feature list, but I cannot infer your security posture from a slick UI. Integration with our existing Microsoft 365 tenant and SSO via Azure AD is also non-negotiable, because a standalone identity silo is just another attack surface I have to manage.

responses.short_answer: 正直めちゃくちゃ興味あります — クライアントとの打ち合わせ中にノート取りながら同時に話を聞くの、本当にしんどくて。でもためらう理由があるとすれば、個人フリーランス向けのプランがなくてエンタープライズ契約しか選択肢がないやつと、日本語の議事録精度がどこまで使えるか、ってとこですかね。
responses.reasoning: 私、クライアント3〜4社と同時に動いてるので、週に結構な数の打ち合わせがあるんですよ。その場でスケッチしながら話を聞きたいのに、片手でノート取ってると完全に集中できなくて、後で『あれどういう話だったっけ』ってなること多くて。AI議事録、あったら本当に助かるなとは思うんですけど、Figmaとかそういうデザインツール系はわりと個人プランがしっかりしてるけど、ミーティング系のSaaSって急に『チーム5名〜』とかになりがちで、そこがちょっと怖い。あと私の打ち合わせ、ほぼ全部日本語なので、英語ベースのモデルが微妙なところで誤変換してたら逆に信用なくしそうというか、クライアントに出すものだから精度がシビアで。

responses.short_answer: If another tax accountant I actually know told me it saved them real time in client meetings, I'd try a free trial. But I'd hesitate a lot before buying — client conversations in our office are confidential financial matters, and I'd need to understand exactly where that audio data goes before I'd let any software record them.
responses.reasoning: Every meeting I have with a client involves their income, their debts, sometimes their family situations. If a tool is transcribing all of that and sending it somewhere — some server I can't locate — that's not a small concern, that's a compliance and trust issue. And then there's the cost question: a monthly subscription sounds small until you're paying it for six people for three years and wondering what you actually got. I've seen software fees add up quietly. I'd also need to know it handles Japanese properly, not just technically but naturally — the way clients actually speak in Hokkaido isn't the same as textbook business Japanese. If someone showed me it works, really works, in our kind of office, I'd listen.

responses.short_answer: If it can cut down my visit report time — honestly even by half — I'd want to try it right away. The thing that would slow me down is whether it's okay to use it under pharma compliance rules, and whether the company would even allow it on our devices.
responses.reasoning: Right now I'm spending like two hours every night in my apartment just writing up visit records from the day — who I saw, what I said, what the doctor's reaction was, all of it into our SFA system. If AI could transcribe and structure that for me from the actual conversation, that's time I get back every single day. But here's the problem: what I say to a doctor in a detail visit is kind of sensitive from a promotional compliance standpoint — there are strict MR rules about what we can and can't claim. So I'd be nervous about whether audio data from those visits is going somewhere it shouldn't be. And honestly, even if I loved the tool personally, I'd need my manager or the company IT team to greenlight it before I could actually use it — I'm not going to go rogue on something like this.

responses.short_answer: I would consider such software only after it had passed through the proper procurement process, received information security certification, and been approved for use by the relevant division heads. What would give me pause — and considerable pause at that — is the prospect of an automated system generating what is, in our office, a legal record of government proceedings.
responses.reasoning: In our division, meeting minutes are not mere summaries — they are official documents subject to information disclosure requests under the Public Records Act, and errors of attribution carry real administrative consequences. I have seen junior staff struggle even with our approved word-processing templates; I cannot imagine deploying an AI system that none of us have been trained on, that has not been vetted by our information systems office, and that was not included in this fiscal year's budget plan. Even if the software were technically capable, the procedural obstacles alone would require at minimum two or three approval cycles and likely a pilot review committee. That is simply the appropriate course.

responses.short_answer: Honestly, I'd try almost any AI meeting notes tool immediately if it could handle Japanese-language meetings accurately — that's the single biggest unlock for enterprise adoption in Japan. What makes me hesitate is the gap between demo performance and real-world reliability, and whether the vendor actually has local support infrastructure to handle enterprise procurement and troubleshooting in Japanese.
responses.reasoning: At Lion Corporation, we run a lot of cross-functional meetings where the output — action items, decisions, follow-up owners — gets lost in translation between the meeting room and the ringi process. If a tool can auto-generate structured minutes in Japanese with reasonable accuracy, that's an immediate, demonstrable ROI I can bring to the executive committee without needing a consultant to build the business case. But I've been burned before: two tools I piloted last year looked phenomenal in the vendor demo, then fell apart on actual meetings because they couldn't handle keigo, overlapping speakers, or industry-specific terminology like '施策' and 'KPI達成率.' The other hesitation is vendor maturity — if there's no Japanese-speaking customer success team, no local data residency option, and no experience navigating Japanese enterprise procurement cycles, the tool is basically a non-starter no matter how good the AI is. I can champion a tool internally, but I can't be the vendor's Japanese customer support desk on top of that.
